title: Impeaching Theodore Chuang, a judge of the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, for high crimes and misdemeanors.
plain_summary: This bill wants to remove Judge Theodore Chuang from his job as a federal judge. Congress says the judge did something wrong when he made a court order that gave USAID employees access back to government computer systems. They believe the judge went beyond what he was allowed to do and got in the way of the President's job of handling foreign policy and keeping the country safe. Congress thinks this was so serious that the judge should be impeached and removed from his position.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution is about impeaching Theodore Chuang, a judge of the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, for high crimes and misdemeanors.

sections.title: House Action
sections.text: On March twenty-fourth, two thousand twenty-five, Representative Ogles and Representative Davidson submitted this resolution to the House of Representatives. The resolution was sent to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Impeachment Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives has decided that Theodore Chuang, a judge of the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, should be impeached for high crimes and misdemeanors. This article of impeachment will be presented to the Senate. The House of Representatives brings this impeachment on behalf of itself and all the people of the United States against Judge Theodore Chuang to maintain and support the charges against him for high crimes and misdemeanors.

sections.title: Article One
sections.text: Theodore Chuang, a judge of the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, acted in ways that go against the trust and confidence placed in him as a Federal judge. On March eighteenth, two thousand twenty-five, Judge Chuang wrote a legal opinion in the case J. Does v. Elon Musk and others. In this opinion, he issued a preliminary injunction, which is a court order that requires someone to do or stop doing something temporarily. He ordered the government to give back access to email, payments, security notifications, and other computer systems to current USAID employees and PSCs (people who work for the government under contracts). He also ordered them to restore deleted emails. By issuing this temporary restraining order that requires giving these employees access to government systems, Judge Chuang wrongly weakened the President's Article Two authority. Article Two of the Constitution gives the President the power to conduct foreign policy for the United States. This clear violation of the separation of powers stops the President from responding to threats to our national security. These threats come from USAID employees who have given money to organizations connected to Foreign Terrorist Organizations. This action is completely inconsistent with serving the United States as a district court judge. Judge Chuang ordered immediate access to government systems without considering the President's duty under Article Two to defend the national security of the United States. He did this in a way that was arbitrary and capricious, meaning he made decisions without good reasons or proper consideration. Judge Chuang ordered this access without thinking about the troubled history of foreign assistance through the United States Agency for International Development, known as USAID. A March two thousand twenty-one GAO report shows that from fiscal year two thousand fifteen until fiscal year two thousand nineteen, USAID did not consistently make sure that subawards for projects in the Gaza Strip and Judea and Samaria followed rules designed to prevent financial support for terrorism. More recently, in November two thousand twenty-four, USAID was found to have paid for hundreds of thousands of meals for al-Qaida affiliated fighters in Syria. While Judge Chuang argued that the Trump Administration's reorganization of USAID caused irreparable harm to the people who sued, he failed to consider that his decisions could easily cause irreparable harm to Americans and American interests. Therefore, Judge Theodore Chuang has acted in a way that shows so little concern for the separation of powers and the national security of the United States that he is guilty of high crimes and misdemeanors. He is unfit to hold the office of Federal judge and should be removed from office.
